title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Prices and Volumes Dip; Momentum Builds Around Clean Fuel Market Policies
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(12th May â€“ 18th May)
  • A total of 214,899 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 26.27% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 47.24% WoW as 664,899 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $58.31/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 0.73% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 7.46% in price, reaching $59.43/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 1.85% WoW; on 20th May, the spot price stood at $57.75/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $57.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 3.31% WoW. D4 and D5 RIN prices declined by 2.75% WoW each. Also, D6 RIN prices decreased by 1.85% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

This week saw a decline in California LCFS trading volumes and Type 1 credit prices, along with spot prices. In a very important development for CA LCFS, on 16th May, the California Air Resources Board (CARB) resubmitted proposed amendments to the Low Carbon Fuel Standard to the Office of Administrative Law (OAL), addressing clarity issues and aligning with board directives. If approved by OAL within 30 working days, the updated LCFS regulation could take effect from 1st July 2025.Â  Last week, The EPA submitted key RFS actions to the White House, including proposed 2026 Renewable Volume Obligations and a partial waiver of the 2024 cellulosic biofuel mandate, with an increase in the 2026 biodiesel mandate. Administrator Lee Zeldin pledged swift rulings on pending small refinery exemptions, impacting the RIN market and biofuel compliance costs. Meanwhile, House Ways and Means committee advanced draft proposals amending the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit by restricting feedstock eligibility to the U.S., Mexico, and Canada, revising lifecycle emissions calculations, extending credits through 2031, and excluding foreign-influenced entities. Washington delivered two key policy updates last week. Washington Governor signed HB 1409, revising the Clean Fuel Standard to target a 45% GHG reduction by 2038, accelerating and intensifying previous goals. Lastly, the Washington Department of Ecology proposed updates to commercial vehicle pollution regulations and the Advanced Clean Trucks rule to accelerate zero-emission truck adoption with increased industry flexibility.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

    [caption id="attachment_597355" align="alignnone" width="998"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.73% WoW fall, decreasing from $58.74 to $58.31 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 7.46% WoW, from $64.22 to $59.43. Type 1 trades saw 214,899 credits exchanged, reflecting a fall of 26.27% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 47.24%, reaching 664,899 credits. Â 

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_597356" align="alignnone" width="1002"]
Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 1.85% WoW, reaching $56.73/MT. On 20th May, the average spot price was $52.88/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $57.00/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_597357" align="alignnone" width="1002"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 49 trades were reported in the market, with 4 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $79.65 for one Type 2 transaction at a volume of 50,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $54.00 per credit of Type 1, reported for one transaction at a volume of 106, which is also the smallest transfer by volume this week. The largest volume transfers of 75,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $58.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_597358" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot price for LCFS Type 1 credits in April was $58.26, holding relatively steady at approximately $58.37 through the week of May 12th. The total number of trading volumes transacted for May stood at 2.32 MMT through the week of 12th May.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_597359" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 12th May - 18th May, total VPT recorded a 11.29% decline WoW, with a fall in all non-zero prices.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 3.31% WoW. D4 and D5 RIN prices declined by 2.75% WoW each. Also, D6 RIN prices decreased by 1.85% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming Dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • CARB Resubmits Proposed Low Carbon Fuel Standard Amendments to OAL: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) resubmitted proposed amendments to the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) to the Office of Administrative Law (OAL), as updated to address clarity issues identified by OAL, as well as to follow board direction and otherwise further improve alignment with the objectives of the rulemaking.
  • House Committees Propose Major Changes to 45Z Clean Fuel Credit and Rescind RFS Funding in Budget Package: On May 12, the House Ways and Means Committee and the House Committee on Energy and Commerce released draft proposals for a reconciliation budget package, dubbed the â€œbig, beautiful billâ€ by former President Trump. The House Ways and Means Committee proposed key amendments to the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit, including restricting eligibility to fuels made from feedstocks grown in the U.S., Mexico, or Canada, and changing how lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions are calculatedâ€”specifically excluding emissions from indirect land use change.
  • US EPA to Rule on Backlog of Small Refinery Biofuel Exemption Requests, Zeldin Says: On May 15, 2025, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) submitted two major Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) actions to the White House Office of Management and Budget for review: the proposed Renewable Volume Obligations (RVOs) for 2026 and beyond, and a final rule to partially waive the 2024 cellulosic biofuel mandate. The EPA is reportedly considering a 2026 biodiesel mandate of 4.65 billion gallons â€” a significant increase from the 3.35 billion gallons in 2025, although still below industry recommendations.
  • Washington Governor Signs Bill to Update the Stateâ€™s Clean Fuel Standard: On May 17, Washington Governor Bob Ferguson signed HB 1409 into law, substantially revising the stateâ€™s Clean Fuel Standard (CFS) by setting a new greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction target of 45% by 2038, relative to 2017 levels. Originally, the CFS, implemented in 2023, aimed for a 20% reduction by 2034. The new legislation accelerates and intensifies these targets, mandating a 5% additional reduction by 2026, 4% by 2027, and 3â€“4% annually through 2038. The Washington Department of Ecology may further increase the target to 55% post-2032 under certain conditions.
  • Ecology Proposes New Updates to Help Industry Transition to Clean Semi-Trucks: The Washington Department of Ecology is proposing updates to regulations that reduce pollution from new semi-trucks and other commercial vehicles sold in the state. When combined with increased state funding, the updates will increase flexibility and help the industry transition to zero-emission trucks. These updates include changes to the Advanced Clean Trucks rule. Under the rule, manufacturers can gradually increase their zero-emission medium- and heavy-duty vehicle sales through 2035, or they can buy credits from other automakers who exceed requirements.
